title: White-Background Catalog Cleanup for a Firearms/Optics/Outdoor Shopify Store
grounding_note: VERBATIM from the source brief: the store "sell[s] thermal cameras, night vision optics, firearms, ammunition, trail camera mounts, and hunting accessories" on Shopify, the owner is "hands-off," and Phase 1 explicitly requires "Audit and clean up product catalog — titles, descriptions, tags, and images." The image-cleanup line is the one design-relevant task in an otherwise ops/data-feed gig, so this benchmark scopes ONLY the product-IMAGE portion of that catalog cleanup (the distributor data-feed/StockSync/MAP/404 work is operations, not Adobe, and is deliberately excluded). REASONABLY ENRICHED to make it runnable: I specify that distributor-supplied product photos arrive inconsistent (off-white/gray seamless, tilted, warm color cast, mixed framing) — a true real-world condition for firearms/optics drop-ship feeds — and that the deliverable must meet a Shopify/marketplace hero spec (pure #FFFFFF background, square 1:1, 2048px, plus a transparent-PNG variant for collection tiles). The specific SKU (a thermal weapon scope), the white-point target, the square canvas, and the logo vectorization are enrichments; none contradicts the brief. No fabricated unrelated deliverable — every output is a "cleaned-up catalog image."
one_line_ask: Take a batch of inconsistent distributor-supplied product photos for a firearms/optics/outdoor Shopify store and turn them into clean, straightened, color-corrected, pure-white-background, square marketplace hero images (plus transparent-PNG tile variants and a vectorized listing badge).
full_brief:
I run a hands-off Shopify store selling thermal cameras, night-vision optics, firearms, ammunition, trail-camera mounts and hunting accessories. My distributor feeds dump product photos that are all over the place: shot on off-white or gray seamless, slightly tilted, warm/yellow color cast, and framed inconsistently — they look unprofessional next to each other on a collection page and several get rejected by marketplace channels that require a pure-white background. As part of the catalog cleanup I need a repeatable Adobe pipeline that takes one raw distributor photo and produces marketplace-ready listing assets, demonstrated end-to-end on my hero SKU (a handheld thermal weapon scope) and ready to repeat across the catalog.

For the hero SKU, starting from the single raw distributor JPEG I provide, deliver: (1) a corrected master — auto-straightened, cropped to the product, exposure/tone normalized, and the warm color cast neutralized; (2) a pure #FFFFFF background version where the scope is isolated and the messy seamless is replaced with solid white per Shopify/Amazon hero spec; (3) a transparent-PNG cut-out of the same scope for collection tiles and bundle mock-ups; (4) a square 1:1 hero produced by cropping the white-background hero to a centered 1:1 square (the source frame is landscape, so the square is a crop of the existing image — no canvas is invented or extended) and then resized/exported at exactly 2048×2048. Also vectorize the small distributor brand badge I supply (a flat single-color logo mark) into a clean SVG so it scales on the listing.

Constraints: backgrounds must be solid pure white (no gradient, no AI-invented scene), the product must not be distorted or recolored beyond honest color-cast correction, and nothing may be composited in or generatively painted onto the product itself. Keep my color/tone edits faithful to the real product (a firearm optic — accuracy matters to buyers).

inputs.name: raw_thermal_scope_distributor.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Photoreal product photograph of a handheld thermal weapon scope (matte black cylindrical body, rubberized eyepiece, control buttons, short Picatinny rail mount), shot on a slightly off-white / pale-gray seamless paper backdrop in a small product studio. Deliberately imperfect distributor-feed look: the whole frame is tilted about 4 degrees clockwise, there is a warm yellow-orange color cast from cheap tungsten lighting, framing is loose with uneven empty margins, mild soft shadow under the scope. Sharp real product detail — visible texture on the rubber, tiny scuffs on the matte finish, real plastic specular highlights, accurate hands-off catalog realism, NOT glossy CGI. 3000x2400, 3:4-ish loose framing.
inputs.realism_notes: Must look like a real drop-ship distributor photo, not a finished studio hero: the tilt, warm cast, gray-ish (non-white) background, and loose framing are the FLAWS the Adobe pipeline must fix, so they must actually be present in the generated input.

inputs.name: distributor_brand_badge.png
inputs.gen_prompt: A flat, single-color (solid dark-charcoal) vector-style brand mark / logo for an optics manufacturer: a simple geometric reticle-and-mountain emblem with a short clean wordmark beneath, no gradients, no photographic texture, crisp edges on a transparent background. Small raster PNG roughly 600x600 as a distributor would email it.
inputs.realism_notes: Flat single-color raster logo is the realistic 'we only have a low-res PNG of the badge' situation; it is the correct input for image_vectorize (raster -> clean SVG), which needs a simple flat mark, not a photo.

outputs.name: scope_master_corrected.jpg
outputs.spec: Straightened, cropped-to-product, auto-toned, color-cast-neutralized master JPEG of the thermal scope, sRGB, high quality. The clean base every other deliverable derives from.

outputs.name: scope_whitebg_hero.jpg
outputs.spec: Pure #FFFFFF solid-background hero of the isolated scope, marketplace-compliant (no gradient, no scene), sRGB JPEG.

outputs.name: scope_cutout.png
outputs.spec: Transparent-background PNG cut-out of the scope for collection tiles / bundle mock-ups, edges clean, alpha channel.

outputs.name: scope_hero_square_2048.jpg
outputs.spec: Square 1:1 Shopify/Amazon hero: the white-background hero is cropped to a centered 1:1 square (a crop of the existing landscape frame — no canvas extension or generation), then resized and exported at exactly 2048x2048, sRGB JPEG.

outputs.name: brand_badge.svg
outputs.spec: Clean scalable SVG of the distributor brand mark vectorized from the flat PNG, for crisp display at any listing size.

connector_workflow.note: Locate/confirm the client-uploaded raw distributor JPEG (raw_thermal_scope_distributor.jpg) in CC storage and get its working reference. Initial asset load — no prior step consumed.
Fix the ~4 degree tilt in the distributor shot. Consumes the loaded raw asset.
Trim the uneven empty margins down to the product bounds after straightening.
Normalize exposure/contrast baseline on the cropped frame.
Cool down the warm tungsten cast toward neutral — honest color-cast correction, not a recolor. This is the corrected MASTER (deliverable 1) and the branch point for all downstream variants.
Auto-select the scope on the corrected master to separate product from the messy seamless.
Invert the subject mask to target the background region only.
Solid-fill the inverted (background) selection with pure #FFFFFF white — marketplace-compliant white-bg hero (deliverable 2). Solid-color fill only; product untouched.
Produce the transparent-PNG cut-out of the scope from the same corrected master, for collection tiles / bundles (deliverable 3).

Crop the landscape white-bg hero to a centered 1:1 square. The square side is taken from the shorter (vertical) dimension of the existing frame, so this is a pure crop of the current canvas — it never grows or pads the canvas and invents no pixels (gen-free). Because the source is a white field around the centered product, the square crop stays fully within the source and remains a clean pure-white background. Replaces the former canvas-extension step.
Resize the 1:1 square crop to exactly 2048x2048 for the Shopify/Amazon hero slot (deliverable 4). Downscale/resample only — no canvas extension.

Vectorize the supplied flat distributor brand badge PNG (distributor_brand_badge.png, the second client input) into a clean SVG (deliverable 5). Consumes a client input asset, not a prior step output — this is a parallel branch loading the second supplied asset.

chaining_note: Single producer-consumer chain rooted at the loaded raw asset. Step 1 loads the raw distributor JPEG; steps 2-5 transform it in series (straighten -> crop -> auto-tone -> temperature) to yield scope_master_corrected, the master that branches two ways: (a) steps 6-7-8 build the subject mask, invert it, and solid-fill the background white to make scope_whitebg_hero, which step 10 crops to a centered 1:1 square (scope_square_cropped) and step 11 resizes to exactly 2048x2048 (scope_hero_square_2048); (b) step 9 removes background for the transparent scope_cutout; both branches consume the master. Step 12 (vectorize) is a parallel branch: it consumes the SECOND client input (the flat brand-badge PNG). Every step after the initial loads consumes a prior step's output (inputs_from set accordingly); no step produces a deliverable from thin air, and no step extends or invents canvas — the square hero is a plain crop-then-resize of the white-bg hero. The marketplace-ready asset set emerges only from the full chain.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon: 12 connector calls, all server=adobe, all [C] headless-confirmed, spanning 2 of the connector's tool groups (A1 raster/tonal/masking/FX-geometry + reframe-crop + vectorize, A4 asset load). 11 distinct Adobe tools (well over the >=6 floor) with a genuine multi-branch output->input dependency graph rather than a flat list. Difficulty comes from sequencing a real retouch pipeline correctly (straighten before crop before tone before color, mask->invert->solid-fill for a spec-compliant pure-white background) and from staying strictly inside capability limits: white background is a SOLID image_fill_area fill (not gen-fill, not bg-replace-by-prompt), the cut-out uses image_remove_background, the square hero uses image_crop_and_resize (crop-then-resize with no canvas extension or generation), and the SVG comes from image_vectorize on a flat logo. No Canva, no Express [W] widget track, no [X] not-available capability (no AI object removal, no compositing, no upscaling, no text-to-image), and no local step — the entire deliverable is achievable Adobe-only and headless.

reverify.banned_capability_check: No banned/generative capability remains. image_crop_and_resize is absent, and the square-hero step no longer relies on any canvas extension: step 10 is image_crop_and_resize cropping the landscape white-bg hero to a centered 1:1 square (the square side comes from the shorter existing dimension, so it is a crop within the current canvas — no growing, padding, or outpainting, and never larger than the source in any dimension), and step 11 is image_crop_and_resize resizing that square to exactly 2048x2048 (resample only). White bg via image_select_subject -> image_invert_selection -> image_fill_area (SOLID #FFFFFF fill within the existing canvas); cut-out via image_remove_background; SVG via image_vectorize on a flat logo. No text-to-image, no from-scratch art, no AI object removal / reconstruction, no upscaling/super-resolution, no generative fill, no bg-replace-by-prompt, and image_fill_area is never used to extend a canvas. image_apply_lens_blur is not used. All 12 steps are server=='adobe', exec_mode [C] headless. Every input a step consumes is declared in inputs[] (raw JPEG + flat brand-badge PNG).
reverify.notes: Fixed the flagged doability defect: former step 10 (image_fill_area 'placing the hero on a solid pure-white square canvas') physically could not square a non-square image, since image_fill_area only solid-fills a selection within the existing canvas. Replaced it with image_crop_and_resize that crops the landscape white-bg hero to a centered 1:1 square (gen-free crop within the existing frame), and step 11 remains image_crop_and_resize to reach exactly 2048x2048. image_fill_area now appears once (step 8) and image_crop_and_resize twice (steps 10-11); distinct_adobe_tools = 11, tool_call_count = 12. Reconciled all stale generative wording (full_brief, the scope_hero_square_2048 output spec, chaining_note, reverify) to describe a crop-then-resize with no 'generative-expanded'/'outpaint'/'canvas extension'. No IP/naming issues (distributor brand badge is a fictional flat mark; Shopify/Amazon are delivery platforms and kept), so brand_rename is empty.
